speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five. Today is January 2, 1997. I'm interviewing Shirley Warman. You said that you were in Tomsk for two years. And why did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tomsk? They tell us, you're free. You could go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian people, the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Ulm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before Ulm, where did you go from Tomsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No, Tomsk, we went to Ulm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you not go to Krasnodor?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Krasnodar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Krasnodar.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Yes, we went to Krasnodar. From Krasnodar we went to Ulm.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, let's talk a little bit about Krasnodar. OK? How did you get to Krasnodar?

speaker: interviewee
text: With [NON-ENGLISH], with the train-- with the-- with the cars-- with the trains-- with the cattle trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: They sent you from Tomsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You went with your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how long did it take you to get to Krasnodar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was happening on the trains, the cattle train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it similar to what happened in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's the same thing, the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Krasnodar?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's still Russia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know in what part of Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the climate always warm, or were there cold periods?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're cold. There must be cold too, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of place was Krasnodar? Was it a camp or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was like a city. If you find a place where to live-- I find a place. I was living with a Russian woman. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do any work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't work either. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage with food and other--

speaker: interviewee
text: They send us packages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain what packages were and who sent it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The packages was shoes, soap, powder milk, cocoa, materials. And we sell-- I have on-- you know, on the black market. We sold it. And we had money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you the packages? How did you get them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was from that organization, from the Bund. People find out where we are, and they send us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the people that found out? Who told them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who told them. They just found out. They're from England. They were in England. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your husband know anybody on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Afterwards, I met him here. Before, we didn't know nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent it to you directly-- you and your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do with what you got?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them I used. Some of them I sold it. And I bought something else.

speaker: interviewer
text: You bought food, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your clothing like when you were in Krasnodar?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had clothes, you know. We buy something. If we have the money, we bought.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living? What type of housing?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was small houses-- very small houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the housing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it just-- simple houses, very simple.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was in there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just had one room with my husband, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other people in the house too?

speaker: interviewee
text: They live in another-- another room-- the Russian-- the Russian-- not with. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else did you do in Krasnodar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a child there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I got pregnant. And I was three days in labor. I couldn't sleep, you know. Because I went-- we were walking to the hospital. It was raining. It was a lot of mud. And I came in. My husband went home. And I was there. And I didn't sleep for three nights. And I was up, and I fell asleep. And my father came in my dream. He said, don't worry. You're gonna have a baby. And I woke up, and I start to get pain. It was cold. I would wear a shawl over my head, and a jacket. And they took me on the table. They just took off my pants. And I delivered a little boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What was the baby's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The baby's name was Yitzhak. Now it's Irving, after my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: February-- 26th of February, 1944. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You're a little teary-eyed now. What are you thinking of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where my life went through. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after the baby was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had nothing to give him-- no diapers. Everybody brought in some old clothes, old, you know, pillow cases. And this was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it cold in the hospital when--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very cold, very cold. When he wet, you know, his behind was frozen to the piece of diaper-- very cold. But afterwards, it got better, you know. It was warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got back to the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was nice-- warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have food for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I fed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You breastfed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Breastfed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is he now, your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, now he's OK. Now is he's in southwest Miami Beach. He has two children. He has two granddaughter-- two grandchildren. He has two daughters and two grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how long after your son was born did you stay in Krasnodar?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed till 1946. Then we went to Ulm.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, everybody went, you know, but they send us. We-- we had-- we couldn't go no-- no place-- the trains-- again with the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. When were you sent? When was the liberation? Or when did you know that the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we knew the war when 1945. We knew the war was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you stayed in Krasnodar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, till 1946, the beginning 1946. And we went to Ulm

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Ulm?

speaker: interviewee
text: By trains. Yeah, it was a lot of DP-- DP camp it was. A lot of people came. It wasn't too bad--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: --there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who sent you there? How did--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- how-- how did they treat the people when the war was over-- the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. Not bad-- very good. Yeah, it was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent you to Ulm?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Ulm, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and that was a DP camp, you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. And then was the UNRRA, the Jewish organizations start with the HIAS there. And they start to give. They brought us clothing, a lot of clothing-- from the dead people, from the Jewish dead people. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Ulm exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ulm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was already Germany. This was Germany-- part, you know, from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do any work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't work, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was nothing. It was good. We didn't work. We had food. They gave us food. They gave us clothing. We had where to wash ourselves, you know. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go after Ulm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was Germany, Stuttgart. And then we find a place where the-- by German-- the-- the-- the people told us where to go. And we went. And the-- a German family was-- used to live there. And we got a room there. And there-- there wasn't-- they didn't live anymore. The Germans didn't live there anymore. And we located there. And we went to eat in the DP camp. You know, the UNRRA send us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The DP camp was Ulm?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- no, in Stuttgart. There was-- yeah. But they got liberated-- the people there. And they start to work already how to come here to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Stuttgart, you said you lived in a German--

speaker: interviewee
text: A German apartment, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did you find in that apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I find shape of lamps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lamp shades?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lamp shades. And there was a-- a sign written on-- this was made from the Jewish skins. Then, I was looking around. I find a piece of soap. And there was a sign made-- the was from the Jewish fat.

speaker: interviewer
text: From fat.

speaker: interviewee
text: From fat from the Jewish body and we made the soap. I went to the Jewish organization. I said, I can't stay over there anymore. They said, there's no place where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you saw what that said--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you felt that you couldn't stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Told-- we were staying. We were there-- I wasn't too long in Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- did you ever get back to Lublin while you were in Stuttgart?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My husband went back. I never went.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went back 1946 when it was the pogrom with the-- you know, the-- in Kielce. They took off the people from the trains. He went back. He went to my parents' house. There was, over there, Polish people were living. They didn't let him in. They said, nobody's alive. He said, if you better go if you know what is good for you. Then he went where he used to-- when his parents used to live. There was no-- nothing left there. It was demolished. Then he went to Majdanek to see what's going on. It was three days. After three days, he came.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- when he went-- you were living in Stuttgart?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you chose not to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I told him not to go either, but he said he must go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when he came back and told you all of this--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very sad. It was very bad. But life goes on. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What decided you to come here? How did you decide to come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whereas I had-- I remember my husband's family lived in-- in Canada, Toronto-- and one family-- his family-- I didn't have nobody-- lived here, in New York. And we start leafing through the Jewish organizations to find out that-- I-- we didn't have any addresses-- to find out the addresses. And we started to write. And the-- the Canadian, they started to send us packages-- food. But we never receive. It was too late. They've given a lot. And from here, we start to receive letters. But you-- you-- you're supposed to have a sponsor who's-- wants to take you down here. But we came through the Bund here. We didn't-- we didn't need a sponsor. So we came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come?

speaker: interviewer
text: What date?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left Stuttgart. I came here the 1st, 1947, April the 1st. But we went. Six weeks we was in Bremen Harbor. There was a strike. We couldn't walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bremen Harbor is where?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Germany-- yes-- near Veen-- in Veen. And-- and they were-- we were there six weeks after, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you staying?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like the soldiers had, you know, the-- but there was good. It was very good. We have place where to-- to get washed. And we had food. And they had treat us very nicely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did it take you to get here from Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three weeks. We went. In the middle of the ocean the one motor got broken. You know, this was a-- this was a military ship. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of it? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name was Ena Pyle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ernie Pyle?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yeah, Ernie Pyle. And then they took us to the break within England. England didn't let us in. Now we was over there, by the-- near the water. We were there, I think, three days. And then they switch another ship. I don't remember the other one. And they start to take women with children and older people, to take first on the ship. And all the men was behind. Everybody start to cry, to scream. After so many times, now we're going to be separated again. But no time-- when they located us, they went for the men. I had-- I had a child. I was in the top cabin, and my husband was sleeping downstairs, you know, in the boiler room with the men. We had food. But listen. We couldn't eat. I was sick three weeks-- throwing up. There was all of people, you know, the religious people-- Hasidim. They had herring. They had-- they gave them onions.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ask, and they give me something. And we arrived over here in Ellis Island. And from there, they took us to the HIAS. Who was there at Ellis Island?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who brought us to the-- from the organization came-- came-- from the Bund came. And they took us to the HIAS. And we were there. Then we called up my husband's family

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their name, your husband's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gold. And they took us there. And-- no. And they came. And there was three days before Passover. And they took us to the first seder night. And they were afraid. You-- if you have family, they have to take you in. So they took in their daughter to live with them. And we were there the first seder night, and we slept over. And then we went back to the HIAS.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came to this country, what did you have with you? Did you have enough clothing for yourself or your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We got right away here. My aunt, she has two daughters. She brought us a lot of clothes, and she brought, for the baby. We bought him shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had no shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had no-- they-- the-- one German woman give him, made him a pair of socks. With this, we came to this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself? I had-- I was wear-- I-- I had-- I didn't have-- I didn't have no underwear, no bras-- you know, bras. But I had a-- a dress and some shoes. Then my aunt gave me some shoes that was too small. And I was wearing-- I couldn't wear them. Then we find a friend of ours-- he had a jewelry packet in Canal Street through a friend from Europe. And he came to HIAS. He took out $50. He give to my husband. He took off his coat. He give it to him to wear. And since then, you know, everybody start to look for work. And I find-- I find an apartment by a woman, a very religious woman. And she gave me her room. And I was paying, I think $10 a month. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this? The room, where-- where were you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cherry Street, downtown, in New York. And I was happy, you know. I got a place. And I went to the union. The union find-- they find me a job. And now-- and I-- the baby was very young. And I took him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your baby when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: When he came here, was two years and two months. I took him every morning to Henry Street. It was a nursery. And I went to work to learn the trade. And my husband went to work, to his making the shoes, you know, to work-- by this. And this was going on. And then, you know, all of a sudden, the baby got sick. And they called me up, and I came home. And he was very sick. And I called up a neighbor. I didn't know how to speak. He had diphtheria. And I call an ambulance. And we went there. And we were sitting there. And nobody showed up. And I see he lays-- he's gonna die. I start to scream. I walked over. It's a noise. And I start to shake him. And I start to-- you know. I spoke Jewish, but she was-- she knew Germans. So she understood what, you know, more than less what I'm saying. And she called right away up a doctor. And the doctor said-- they took me, my husband. And they took us-- near the ward there's a special-- a hospital. It was just for this. I don't know the name of the hospital. And they took him in. They let us out. We didn't know how to go home. And over we're sitting till the morning. And the morning desk people had to go home. We went home. And I went to work. And he went to work. Then they called us up. He's very, very, sick. And I didn't know what to do. And I didn't know where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I called the union and the-- my boss called the union. The union told him. And they met. And we went to see him. And it took two-- two weeks. Then we took him home. He was OK. Did you have any other children here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then we start both to work, and we saved up some money. And-- and the-- we bought a four-family house in Bensonhurst. And Irving was already 10 and a 1/2-- 11 1/2 years old. And he start to cry. He said, ma, I'm all alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, everybody has family-- grandpas, aunts. And I said to my husband, I said, what we gonna do? Who could afford it? Now we bought this house too. It's very bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got pregnant. And I was working till the seventh month. And I got Karl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was Karl born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Karl was born 19-- 1957, in Borough Park, in Maimonides Hospital. It was a very big thrill.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Karl now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Karl's-- Karl lives-- now he's in Florida with his brother-- with the whole family. He works very hard, and he lives in Long Island. He has two beautiful children. Jonathan is fourteen. Danielle is-- he's a genius, a walking encyclopedia. And he is 100 from all the subjects. And Danielle is just 10. She's very beautiful. She's very good. And Irving has two children. One lives in Houston, Texas with her--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name or his name, your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Jennifer, and his name is Brent. And they bought a house. He-- he works in the computer chips-- for the making the computer chips. And she works over there too, but I don't know in the office someplace. And Irving has two daughters. And she's married. And she has now two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his-- what's the daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beth. And the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her last name? Jaramillo. And her husband is Andy. And she has two little girls. One's gonna be-- January is gonna be three years old. She-- her name is Deborah. And the little one is Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old is Rachel?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's-- I don't know-- a month? Two? Not even two. Now the whole family is together. Except he said, ma, Jennifer was there. He-- she-- they came for a week-- just missing you. And I said, all right. Soon I'm gonna see you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I know that you're a widow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your-- your husband pass away?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1973. He woked up in the house. This was Labor Day weekend. I went to work, I went to work earlier than him. While I worked in Manhattan I have to take a bus and a train. And he was working Canal Street. And I said, OK. I see you. I see you at night-- tonight. And my-- Irving had a bungalow in the mountains. And the wife was standing there-- staying there with the two children. So, and for the weekend, he said he's gonna go up.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 2 o'clock a guy came over. And he said, are you Mrs. Warman? I said, yes. I said, what's the matter? He said to me, your son is here. He wants to see you. I said, oh my God. Why did he came? It's-- it's nice thing he came to mother to say goodbye. He's going to his family for the weekend. When I-- when I got up, I saw my boss standing near my son. And my boss is crying. And I saw my son. I said, what happened? He-- he couldn't talk, but my-- my boss said it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Benny got killed in an elevator. He didn't know the elevator didn't work. He walked up three flights. He's supposed to walk up another flight or two. I don't remember. And there was a little window in the elevator. He wants to see if the-- what's going on. And somebody pushed a button, and he got killed. And this was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The elevator started to move?

speaker: interviewee
text: It started to move, yes. And then, you know, we went home. We took a cab. My boss closed up the shop right away. Everybody went to work. I went home. This was Labor Day weekend. And the following day, a friend of mines-- he lives here. He's my landsman. [NON-ENGLISH]-- his name was [NON-ENGLISH]. He took both boys to recognize the body. They didn't let me go. And they recognized the body. Well, Karl run away. He couldn't take it. And this was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your husband like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, full of life. All the neighbors knew. He always was singing. And with everybody-- just self-learning-- he never went to school. He could have a speech in English. And he loved sport. He was full of life. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you after his death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: After his death.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Look. It was very bad. Irving went to Florida. He was a stock broker with a family. And I was alone. And Karl just-- supposed to go away to-- to college in Long Island-- Stony Brook. He couldn't go. And then I told him, look. You've got to go. You gotta. No, you've got to go to school. But we talked. And, you know. After two weeks, I went back to work. And he came when he could. He had a ride. He came home. And this was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you live your life now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. Not-- I have the pleasure of my children. I have a very fine friend. I have a good friend, yes. We go together. We, you know. We're going to Florida now. Yes, he's a good friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Hershel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you think of the Holocaust often? Today, do you think of the Holocaust often in your present life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- I don't understand what you mean. I didn't understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. But what did-- what did you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: I asked if you think a lot about the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK. And we'll continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.
